kings_day.intro: King's Day on 27 April celebrates the Dutch monarch's birthday and turns the whole country orange.
kings_day.history: The Netherlands has marked a royal day since 1885, long as ‚Queen’s Day‘ on 30 April. When Willem-Alexander became king in 2013, it moved to his birthday, 27 April, and was renamed King’s Day.
kings_day.traditions: People dress in orange, the national colour, and cities host street parties, concerts and canal boats packed with revellers — Amsterdam especially. A nationwide free ‚vrijmarkt‘ (flea market) lets anyone sell second-hand goods on the street.
funFacts: Everyone wears orange, after the royal House of Orange-Nassau.
The ‚vrijmarkt‘ lets anyone sell goods tax-free for the day.
It was 'Queen's Day' on 30 April until 2013.

liberation_day.intro: Liberation Day on 5 May marks the end of the Nazi occupation of the Netherlands in 1945 and celebrates freedom.
liberation_day.history: On 5 May 1945 German forces in the Netherlands surrendered, ending five years of occupation in the Second World War. The previous evening, 4 May, is the national Remembrance Day for the war’s victims; the 5th then celebrates liberation and freedom.
liberation_day.traditions: The day is opened by the king and features ‚Liberation Festivals‘ with open-air concerts across the country. It follows the solemn two minutes of silence held nationwide on the evening of 4 May.
funFacts: Remembrance (4 May) and Liberation (5 May) form a linked pair.
It is an official day off only once every five years.
Liberation Festivals are held in cities across the country.
